era_context: The early 15th century continues with the Council of Constance in session (1414–1418), addressing the Great Schism and heresy. The Hussite movement is gaining momentum in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is ongoing, with English forces under Henry V making significant gains in Normandy. The Ming Dynasty is consolidating power in China, and Portuguese maritime exploration is beginning to expand into the Atlantic.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited European documentation)

events.title: Council of Constance: Condemnation of Jerome of Prague
events.description: In 1416, the Council of Constance condemned Jerome of Prague, a close associate of Jan Hus, for heresy. Jerome was executed on 30 May 1416, following a trial that mirrored that of Jan Hus (executed 1415). Jerome's condemnation further galvanised the Hussite movement in Bohemia and demonstrated the council's commitment to suppressing religious reform. The executions of both Hus and Jerome became martyrdom narratives that fuelled the Hussite Wars.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, patron of the council)

sources.name: Council of Constance, official acts and decrees
Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronicle of the Council of Constance

events.certainty_note: The condemnation and execution of Jerome of Prague on 30 May 1416 is well-documented in official council records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English Consolidation in Normandy
events.description: In 1416, English forces under Henry V were consolidating their control of Normandy following the Battle of Agincourt (1415). The English were systematically capturing Norman towns and fortifications, establishing English control over the region. This campaign demonstrated the effectiveness of English military tactics and logistics. By 1416, most of Normandy was under English control, and Henry V was preparing to advance further into France.
events.certainty_note: The English consolidation of Normandy in 1416 is well-documented in contemporary English and French chronicles.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Forbidden City Construction Continues
events.description: In 1416, construction of the Forbidden City in Beijing continued under the direction of the Yongle Emperor. The massive palace complex, commissioned in 1406, was nearing completion. The construction involved hundreds of thousands of workers and demonstrated the Ming Dynasty's wealth, power, and organisational capabilities. The Forbidden City represented the pinnacle of Ming architectural achievement and would serve as the imperial residence and seat of government for centuries.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Emperor, patron of the project)
events.certainty_note: The construction of the Forbidden City is well-documented in Ming records. The dates (1406–1420) are well-established.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Early Atlantic Voyages
events.description: In 1416, Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, were conducting early Atlantic voyages to explore the African coast and Atlantic islands. These voyages were laying the groundwork for the Age of Exploration and establishing Portuguese maritime dominance. The exact details of specific voyages in 1416 are unclear, but the period marks the beginning of systematic Portuguese exploration.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Portuguese exploration is well-attested, but specific voyages in 1416 are not clearly documented. This entry describes the era context rather than a discrete event.

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Consolidation under Mehmed I
events.description: In 1416, Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) was consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Ottoman state was expanding its territorial control and military capabilities, setting the stage for later expansion. This period saw the development of Ottoman administrative structures and the refinement of the Janissary corps. The Ottoman Empire was becoming a major regional power, though it had not yet challenged Byzantine Constantinople directly.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 1416 are not distinctly documented. This entry describes the era context rather than a discrete event.

disconfirming_evidence: The execution of Jerome of Prague is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward heresy trial, but it involved complex political negotiations and was influenced by the power dynamics of the council. The English consolidation of Normandy is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory, but it involved complex negotiations, local resistance, and significant civilian suffering. The Forbidden City's construction is sometimes attributed to a single architect, but it was a collaborative effort involving many craftsmen and officials.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1416 is moderate in quality. European events (Council of Constance, Hundred Years' War) are well-documented from multiple perspectives. Chinese records are detailed but reflect imperial priorities. Ottoman records are available but less detailed in European archives. Sub-Saharan African and American events are largely absent from surviving written records. The Council of Constance records are particularly rich, as they were official proceedings with multiple contemporary chroniclers. The Forbidden City construction is well-documented in Ming administrative records.

graph_edges.note: The execution of Jan Hus in 1415 was followed by the execution of Jerome of Prague in 1416, both at the Council of Constance
The executions of Hus and Jerome at the Council of Constance catalysed the Hussite Wars, including the Battle of Vitkov Hill in 1419
The English consolidation of Normandy in 1416 led to further conquest in 1417
